summary: Japanese architect (born 1957), 2014 Pritzker Prize winner, known for using paper tubes and recycled materials for humanitarian disaster relief housing. Key buildings include the Cardboard Cathedral in Christchurch, New Zealand, and the Centre Pompidou-Metz. He built the Takatori Catholic Church ('Paper Dome') in Kobe after the 1995 earthquake, designed the Japanese Pavilion at Expo 2000 with Frei Otto, and created the Paper Partition System for disaster victims.

works.name: Cardboard Cathedral, Christchurch
works.description: Transitional cathedral built for the Anglican Diocese of Christchurch after the 2011 earthquake destroyed the original. Eight shipping containers form the walls; the roof is a triangular polycarbonate structure. Cardboard tubes were stacked on top of the shipping containers. A paper tube studio used in the design process was incorporated into the final design. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Transitional cathedral in Christchurch built after the 2011 earthquake; eight shipping containers form the walls; triangular polycarbonate roof; cardboard tubes stacked on top of shipping containers
examples: This architect won the 2014 Pritzker Prize for his design of the Cardboard Cathedral in Christchurch.
Eight shipping containers form the walls of this architect's Cardboard Cathedral in Christchurch.
tubes of which were stacked on top of shipping containers by Shigeru Ban to create a cathedral after the 2011 Christchurch earthquake

works.name: Centre Pompidou-Metz
works.description: The Centre Pompidou-Metz in northeastern France (in the city of Metz, the most populous city in Lorraine, located at the confluence of the Moselle and Grand Seille rivers). Ban designed its distinctive roof: an undulating hexagonal shell of glue-laminated timber (also described as curving fiberglass), inspired by a Chinese bamboo hat that Ban found in Paris. A paper tube studio used to develop the design was incorporated into the final building. The museum also features a glass elevator inspired by a gondola, from which visitors 'ski' down to the galleries. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Roof inspired by a Chinese bamboo hat (found in Paris); undulating/hexagonal shell of glue-laminated timber; paper tube studio incorporated into the final design
examples: A bamboo hat inspired the hexagonal shell made from timber he used for the roof of a Metz art museum.
Its complex undulating roof was inspired by a Chinese hat that the architect found in Paris.

A paper tube studio used to design one of these buildings was incorporated into the final design; the design for the roof of that one of these buildings was inspired by a Chinese hat found by Shigeru Ban.

clues.clue: Glass elevator inspired by a gondola; visitors 'ski' down to the galleries
examples: An art museum designed by this man has a glass elevator inspired by a gondola from which patrons then 'ski' down to the galleries.

clues.clue: Located in Metz, the most populous city in the Lorraine region of northeastern France, at the confluence of the Moselle and Grand Seille rivers
examples: This city, the most populous in the Lorraine region, is located at the confluence of the Moselle and Grand Seille rivers. It is also home to a Georgia Tech campus, as well as a Shigeru Ban-designed Centre Pompidou.

works.name: Humanitarian / Paper Tube Work
works.description: Shigeru Ban is renowned for using paper tubes as cheap, recyclable building materials after natural disasters. Following the 1995 Kobe earthquake (Great Hanshin earthquake), he built refugee shelters and the Takatori Catholic Church ('Paper Dome') in Kobe, which was later moved to Taomi Village in Taiwan in 1999. He also substituted aluminum rods with cardboard tubes for refugee camps in Byumba, Rwanda. He designed an Expo 2000 pavilion (with Frei Otto) whose roof was made entirely of paper/cardboard tubes in a honeycomb shape. He also developed the Paper Partition System for temporary housing for earthquake victims. A 2007 Artek pavilion in Finland was also built from paper waste products. The New York Times critic Michael Kimmelman called him an 'accidental environmentalist.' He stresses 'invisible structure' in his work.

clues.clue: Uses paper tubes and recycled/recyclable materials for disaster relief housing and refugee shelters; Paper Dome (Takatori Catholic Church) in Kobe moved to Taomi Village, Taiwan in 1999 after the 1995 Great Hanshin earthquake
examples: This man's own summer house on Lake Yamanaka was one of the first to use paper for a building material; he subsequently used paper for his series of refugee houses following the Kobe earthquake.
A circular church built from this material after the Great Hanshin Earthquake was shipped to Taomi Village in Taiwan in 1999.
In Kobe, Ban constructed Paper Houses to serve as temporary shelters.

examples: That building, the Villa Mairea, led to the founding of a company here [Finland] whose 2007 pavilion was built out of paper waste products by Japanese architect Shigeru Ban.

works.name: Other Works / General
works.description: Won the 2014 Pritzker Prize (established in 1979 by a co-founder of the Hyatt hotel chain; Toyo Ito won in 2013). Designed transparent toilets for the 2020 Tokyo Olympics whose walls turn opaque when occupied ('smart glass'). Designed the Aspen Museum of Art (his first US museum; formerly housed in a converted hydroelectric plant). The Nomadic Museum was designed for photographer Gregory Colbert's Ashes and Snow exhibition. Some structures anchored by sand-filled Kirin beer crates. His summer house on Lake Yamanaka was among the first buildings to use paper tubes. Often described as a 'disaster architect.' Stresses 'invisible structure.'

comprehensive_summary:
Shigeru Ban (born 1957) is a Japanese architect known for using paper tubes and other recycled or lightweight materials for humanitarian disaster relief housing and temporary structures. He won the 2014 Pritzker Prize (an annual architecture prize established in 1979 by a co-founder of the Hyatt hotel chain; the preceding year's winner was Toyo Ito of the Sendai Mediatheque). Ban is often described as a 'disaster architect' who stresses 'invisible structure,' and the New York Times critic Michael Kimmelman called him an 'accidental environmentalist.'

His most internationally recognized project is the Cardboard Cathedral in Christchurch, New Zealand, built after the 2011 earthquake destroyed the original Anglican cathedral. The transitional building uses eight shipping containers as its walls, with cardboard tubes stacked on top, and a triangular polycarbonate roof.

For the Centre Pompidou-Metz in the city of Metz (the most populous city in the Lorraine region of northeastern France, at the confluence of the Moselle and Grand Seille rivers), Ban designed a distinctive undulating roof of glue-laminated timber — also described as a curving fiberglass structure — with a hexagonal pattern inspired by a Chinese bamboo hat that Ban found in Paris. A paper tube studio he used during the design process was incorporated into the final building. The museum also features a glass elevator inspired by a gondola, from which visitors 'ski' down to the galleries.

Ban's humanitarian work is central to his reputation. After the 1995 Kobe earthquake (Great Hanshin earthquake), he designed refugee houses using paper tubes and built the Takatori Catholic Church ('Paper Dome') in Kobe; that circular church was later shipped to Taomi Village in Taiwan in 1999. He substituted aluminum rods with cardboard tubes for refugee camps in Byumba, Rwanda. He developed the Paper Partition System for temporary housing for earthquake victims. He collaborated with German engineer Frei Otto on the Japanese Pavilion at Expo 2000 in Hanover, which had a roof made entirely of cardboard tubes shaped like a honeycomb. He also built a 2007 pavilion in Finland for the furniture company Artek out of paper waste products.

Other notable works include transparent toilets for the 2020 (Covid-delayed) Tokyo Olympics, whose glass walls turn opaque when occupied using 'smart glass'; the Aspen Museum of Art (his first US museum, replacing a facility formerly housed in a converted hydroelectric plant); and the Nomadic Museum for photographer Gregory Colbert's Ashes and Snow exhibition. Some of his structures are anchored by sand-filled Kirin beer crates. His own summer house on Lake Yamanaka was among the first buildings to use paper tubes as a structural material.
